channel.title: NPR Topics: Education
channel.description: We've been to school. We know how education works. Right? In fact, many aspects of learning — in homes, at schools, at work and elsewhere — are evolving rapidly, along with our understanding of learning. Join us as we explore how learning happens.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: July 1 brings big student loan changes. Here's what you need to know
item.description: A popular (and generous) repayment plan ends, two new plans begin and many borrowers will see new loan limits.
item.content:encoded: A popular (and generous) repayment plan ends, two new plans begin and many borrowers will see new loan limits.
item.dc:creator: Cory Turner

item.title: After years of declines, young students show gains in reading and math
item.description: Unscathed by pandemic-era school closures, the nation's 9-year-olds showed progress in math and reading. It's a different story for 13-year-olds, however.
item.content:encoded:
Unscathed by pandemic-era school closures, the nation's 9-year-olds showed progress in math and reading. It's a different story for 13-year-olds, however.

(Image credit: Olivier Touron)
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: Sweden set to ban mobile phones in schools
item.description: Long championed as a leader in adopting digital technology, Sweden is set to ban mobile phones in schools beginning in the fall for the next academic year.
item.content:encoded:
Long championed as a leader in adopting digital technology, Sweden is set to ban mobile phones in schools beginning in the fall for the next academic year.

(Image credit: James Brooks)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: In these schools, the World Cup is a teachable moment
item.description: One school district outside Boston is turning the World Cup into a teachable moment, with elementary classes learning about different countries' languages, food and wildlife.
item.content:encoded: One school district outside Boston is turning the World Cup into a teachable moment, with elementary classes learning about different countries' languages, food and wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Suevon Lee

item.title: UC Berkeley professor Zvezdelina Stankova explains push to reinstate standardized exams
item.description: NPR's Eyder Peralta speaks with University of California, Berkeley math professor Zvezdelina Stankova about efforts to bring back standardized exams as part of the admissions process.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Eyder Peralta speaks with University of California, Berkeley math professor Zvezdelina Stankova about efforts to bring back standardized exams as part of the admissions process.
item.dc:creator: Eyder Peralta

item.title: Most K-12 teachers say AI's impact on education will eclipse the internet or computers
item.description: A new NPR/Ipsos poll shows many teachers are using AI to save time, but a majority are also worried the technology is making it harder for students to learn to think for themselves.
item.content:encoded: A new NPR/Ipsos poll shows many teachers are using AI to save time, but a majority are also worried the technology is making it harder for students to learn to think for themselves.
item.dc:creator: Lee V. Gaines

item.title: Screens are leaving schools fast, though some students with disabilities rely on them
item.description: Some students with disabilities rely on assistive technology to learn, and they worry it could be swept up in the movement to get screens out of schools.
item.content:encoded:
Some students with disabilities rely on assistive technology to learn, and they worry it could be swept up in the movement to get screens out of schools.

(Image credit: Jonaki Mehta)
item.dc:creator: Jonaki Mehta

item.title: How Trout in the Classroom program brings hands-on ecology directly to students
item.description: As teachers seek ways to keep students engaged in learning, a 30-year-old program offers one solution: raising tanks of trout right in the classroom. May say it's a perfect blend of hands-on science and fun.
item.content:encoded: As teachers seek ways to keep students engaged in learning, a 30-year-old program offers one solution: raising tanks of trout right in the classroom. May say it's a perfect blend of hands-on science and fun.
item.dc:creator: Austin Amestoy

item.title: Shrey Parikh, 14, wins the Scripps Spelling Bee after a nail-biting 'spell-off'
item.description: This is the 101st year of the national spelling competition, and the third time it's been decided by a rapid-fire "spell-off" since the practice was introduced in 2021.
item.content:encoded:
This is the 101st year of the national spelling competition, and the third time it's been decided by a rapid-fire "spell-off" since the practice was introduced in 2021.

(Image credit: Allison Robbert)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: This big university system is embracing AI. Students and faculty aren't all on board
item.description: The California State University system offers an early look at what happens when an administration commits to a technology that its own community isn't convinced will improve education.
item.content:encoded: The California State University system offers an early look at what happens when an administration commits to a technology that its own community isn't convinced will improve education.
item.dc:creator: Lee V. Gaines
